title: 1  Basics
text:
1.1 Quick overview
Given a set of observations of variables, we would like to understand the relationships among these variables.
The standard procedure for developing a statistical model is as follows:
There are two primary goals and related ways to assess the model:
Both Statistical Learning and specific methods like Regression Analysis may use both assessment strategies, but the emphasis differs. In this course, the focus is on the principles of modeling and statistical inference, with prediction discussed but not treated as the primary objective.

title: 1  Basics
text:
Proposing a model informed by exploratory data analysis and relevant domain knowledge.
Estimating the parameters of the proposed model using the available data.
Assessing the adequacy and quality of the fitted model.
Revising the model based on the assessment results.



Prediction (Generalization): We evaluate the fitted model on unseen data, using metrics like MSE or classification error. This assesses the model’s generalization ability, which is the primary focus of subjects like Statistical Learning or Machine Learning.
Explanation (Inference): We use measures such as \(R^2\) to assess the goodness-of-fit of the fitted model, and we use confidence intervals and p-values to perform statistical inference about population parameters. Although these calculations are based on the observed sample, they are interpreted as statements about the population. This approach focuses on understanding relationships among variables and is central to traditional Statistical Modeling, including Regression Analysis.

title: 1  Basics
text:
1.2 Population vs Sample

Population: the set of all elements of interest in a particular study.
Sample: is a subset of the population
Statistical Inference: The process of using data collected on a sample to draw conclusions about a population is called statistical inference.

A population is described by parameters (e.g., \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\beta\)). A sample produces statistics (e.g., \(\bar X\), \(s\), \(\hat\beta\)), which vary across repeated samples. Statistical inference uses the sampling distribution of these statistics to draw conclusions about population parameters. This part will be revisited later in ?sec-inference.

Example 1.1 (gssr)  


Click to expand.

According to a previous national media report, the average age of U.S. adults who watch national TV news is 60 years. A data analyst hypothesizes that the average age of frequent TV-news watchers is greater than 60. To test this, she uses the General Social Survey (GSS), accessible via the gssr R package [1], to draw a sample of 500 respondents and record their ages.

Describe the population: All U.S. adults who watch TV news several times a week or more often, as defined by the GSS survey question on TV-news frequency.
Describe the sample: A random sample of 500 GSS respondents who meet the above criterion and have valid age data.
Sample size: \(n = 500\)
Describe the statistical inference: We want to determine whether the mean age of frequent TV-news watchers is greater than 60 based on the sampled data.

title: 1  Basics
text:
1.3 Data structure
A dataset is a collection of values. Values are organized in two ways. Every value belongs to a variable and an observation.

Definition 1.1 (Variables and Observations [2])  

A variable contains all values that measure the same underlying attribute (like height, temperature, duration) across units.
An observation contains all values measured on the same unit (like a person, or a day, or a race) across attributes.



Example 1.2 (possum)  


Click to expand.

Researchers collected body measurements for bushtail possums in Eastern Australia. They trapped 104 possums and recorded location where possum was trapped, age, gender, head length, skull width, total length, tail length, etc. for each possum. For more details see ?sec-app_possum.

library(DAAG)
data(possum)

Definition 1.2 (Quantitative and Qualitative [3])  

Quantitative data are observations measured on a naturally occurring numerical scale. It is also called numerical data.
Qualitative data are nonnumerical data that can only be classified into one of a group of categories. It is also called categorical data.


In regression, qualitative variables must be encoded using indicator (dummy) variables.

Definition 1.3 (Discrete and Continuous [3])  

Discrete random variable: A random variable that assumes either a finite number of values or an infinite sequence of values such as 0, 1, 2…
Continuous random variable: A random variable that may assume any numerical value in an interval or collection of intervals.







graph TD
    A[All Variables] --> B[Numerical]
    A --> C[Categorical]
    B --> D[Continuous]
    B --> E[Discrete]












NoteRandom variables



A variable in a dataset can be modeled by a random variable. The probability density function / probability mass function of the random variable can describe the distribution of all possible values of the variable in a dataset.
Making a measurement is equivalent to taking a sample from the corresponding random variable. This will be discussed in detail in ?sec-inference.

title: 1  Basics
text:
1.4 Data Visualization

1.4.1 Qualitative (categorical) data
Usually the most important is the class relative frequency: \[
\text{class relative frequency}=\frac{\text{class frequency}}{n}.
\]
To display it, we could use table, bar chart or pie chart.

Example 1.3 (possum)  


Click to expand.

Here we still consider the possum dataset.

library(DAAG)
data(possum)
head(possum)
##     case site Pop sex age hdlngth skullw totlngth taill footlgth earconch  eye
## C3     1    1 Vic   m   8    94.1   60.4     89.0  36.0     74.5     54.5 15.2
## C5     2    1 Vic   f   6    92.5   57.6     91.5  36.5     72.5     51.2 16.0
## C10    3    1 Vic   f   6    94.0   60.0     95.5  39.0     75.4     51.9 15.5
## C15    4    1 Vic   f   6    93.2   57.1     92.0  38.0     76.1     52.2 15.2
## C23    5    1 Vic   f   2    91.5   56.3     85.5  36.0     71.0     53.2 15.1
## C24    6    1 Vic   f   1    93.1   54.8     90.5  35.5     73.2     53.6 14.2
##     chest belly
## C3   28.0    36
## C5   28.5    33
## C10  30.0    34
## C15  28.0    34
## C23  28.5    33
## C24  30.0    32

The variable Pop (location where possum was trapped) is qualitative. We could use the following ways to display it. We first compute the frequency table of the variable Pop:

table(possum$Pop)
## 
##   Vic other 
##    46    58

or relative frequency table:

table(possum$Pop)/length(possum$Pop)
## 
##       Vic     other 
## 0.4423077 0.5576923

Then we could draw the barplot of this variable:

barplot(table(possum$Pop))








and the pie plot:

pie(table(possum$Pop))








Note that table is handling the statistics, while barplot and pie draw on top of the result from table.




1.4.2 Quantitative (numerical) data
For quantitative data, we would like to see the histogram, as well as computing some statistics: min, max, quartiles, median and mean.

Histogram is an approximation of the distribution. In other words, we split the range into small segments (called bins), and count the frequency or relative frequency of data falling into these bins.
Box plot consists of a box, two lines and possibly some points:

The box in the box plot extends from the lower quartile to the upper quartile. The difference between the upper quartile and the lower quartile is called the inter-quartile range (IQR).
The lines, known as whiskers, extend to one and a half times the interquartile range, but they stop at the most extreme data points that fall within this range.
The points, considered as outliers, are those which are not covered by the box and the lines.


Histograms and boxplots help check normality and outliers before fitting regression. These summaries approximate the underlying distribution of the variable. In regression, we later apply similar tools to the residuals to check model assumptions.

Example 1.4 (possum)  


Click to expand.

We still consider the possum dataset. We could use summary to compute the major statistics.

data(possum)
summary(possum)

The variable hdlngth (head length) is quantitative. We first show the histogram. We could use breaks to control the number of bins. Note that the function hist does not only draw the histogram; it also provides many useful pieces of information.

res <- hist(possum$hdlngth, breaks=10)







res
## $breaks
##  [1]  82  84  86  88  90  92  94  96  98 100 102 104
## 
## $counts
##  [1]  1  6  2 12 22 27 22  7  3  0  2
## 
## $density
##  [1] 0.004807692 0.028846154 0.009615385 0.057692308 0.105769231 0.129807692
##  [7] 0.105769231 0.033653846 0.014423077 0.000000000 0.009615385
## 
## $mids
##  [1]  83  85  87  89  91  93  95  97  99 101 103
## 
## $xname
## [1] "possum$hdlngth"
## 
## $equidist
## [1] TRUE
## 
## attr(,"class")
## [1] "histogram"

Then we could show the box plot. You may compare the lines in the plot with the summary table and the histogram above.

boxplot(possum$hdlngth)












1.4.3 Relations among multiple variables
We could show the relation between two variables in a scatterplot. Scatterplots help us assess linearity, detect outliers, and decide whether transformation is needed.

Example 1.5 (possum)  


Click to expand.

When both variables are numerical and continuous:

plot(possum$hdlngth, possum$skullw)








When one variable is categorical:

plot(as.factor(possum$Pop), possum$skullw)








Note that in this case, the categorical data has to be a factor. And once it is cast into a factor, the plot is multiple box plots for each category. This type of grouped boxplot corresponds to comparing group means and is directly related to one-way ANOVA and regression with dummy variables. It will be discussed in later sections.
We can see pair plots for each pair of variables. Pair plots are very important because they help reveal relationships among predictors. Note that before creating the plot, we have to cast pop and sex into factors.

possum$Pop <- as.factor(possum$Pop)
possum$sex <- as.factor(possum$sex)
pairs(possum)














[1] Healy, K. (2025). Gssrdoc: General social survey documentation for use in r.


[2] Wickham, H. (2014). Tidy data. Journal of Statistical Software 59.


[3] Mendenhall, W. and Sincich, T. (2020). A second course in statistics: Regression analyisis. Pearson Education, Inc, [Hoboken, New Jersey].

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.1 What Is Statistical Learning?
Statistical learning studies how to use data to understand relationships and make predictions. In most applications, we observe a response variable \(Y\) and a set of predictors \(X = (X_1, \dots, X_p)\), and we want to learn how \(Y\) depends on \(X\).
A common abstract representation is
\[
Y = f(X) + \varepsilon,
\]
where \(f\) is the systematic part of the relationship and \(\varepsilon\) is random noise.
The main goals are usually:

What is statistical learning?

Prediction vs explanation
Bias–variance tradeoff

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
11.2 Teaching Goals
Students should understand that the main goal of statistical learning is good prediction on new data, not simply fitting the observed sample as closely as possible.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
11.3 Suggested Emphasis

Introduce the overall modeling problem first.
Use simple real-world examples such as house prices, medical diagnosis, or spam filtering.
Make overfitting a central theme from the beginning.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)

Linear regression as a prediction tool
Interpreting fitted relationships
Strengths and weaknesses of linear models
Linear models as a baseline for comparison

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
12.2 Teaching Goals
Students should see linear regression as the starting point of predictive modeling: simple, interpretable, and useful, but limited in flexibility.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
12.3 Suggested Emphasis

Present linear regression mainly as a predictive model.
Keep inference light unless needed for context.
Highlight when linear models fail to capture nonlinear structure.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)

Flexible regression methods
K-nearest neighbors regression

Model flexibility and overfitting

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)

13.2 Teaching Goals
Students should learn that more flexible models can better capture complicated patterns, but at the cost of increased variance.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
13.3 Suggested Emphasis

Connect this unit directly to bias–variance tradeoff.
Compare flexible and inflexible models visually.
Use plots to show underfitting and overfitting.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)

14.1 Main Topics

Classification as a prediction problem
Logistic regression
Decision boundaries
K-nearest neighbors classification

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)

Students should understand that classification uses the same modeling logic as regression, but with categorical outcomes.

Stress the difference between predicting a class and predicting a numeric response.
Introduce class probabilities early.
Use confusion matrices and classification error as evaluation tools.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)

Logistic regression in more detail
Interpreting probabilities
Thresholds and classification decisions
Comparing classifiers

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)

15.2 Teaching Goals
Students should learn how binary classification models convert predictors into class probabilities and final decisions.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
15.3 Suggested Emphasis

Distinguish between probability estimation and hard classification.
Discuss how different thresholds change predictions.
Use practical examples such as disease detection or spam filtering.

LDA
QDA
Naive Bayes

Students should understand the idea of modeling class distributions directly and then using Bayes’ rule for prediction.

Compare logistic regression with LDA and QDA conceptually.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
17.1 Main Topics

Validation set approach
Cross-validation
Bootstrap
Estimating test error

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
17.2 Teaching Goals
This unit is the conceptual center of the course. Students must understand that training error is not enough, and that model performance must be evaluated using principled estimates of test error.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
17.3 Suggested Emphasis

Make cross-validation a central practical tool.
Show why naive comparison by training error is misleading.
Use repeated examples across multiple methods.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
18.1 Main Topics

Ridge regression
Lasso
Elastic net
Shrinkage and complexity control

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
18.2 Teaching Goals
Students should understand how regularization controls complexity and improves prediction without exhaustively searching across models.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
18.3 Suggested Emphasis

Present regularization as a modern answer to overfitting in linear models.
Compare subset selection and shrinkage conceptually.
Use coefficient path plots when possible.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
19.1 Main Topics

Decision trees
Bagging
Random forests
Boosting

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
19.2 Teaching Goals
Students should learn that combining many unstable or weak learners can create a stronger predictive model.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
19.3 Suggested Emphasis

Start with a single tree and its limitations.
Then motivate bagging as variance reduction.
Finally introduce random forests and boosting as improved ensemble ideas.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
20.1 Main Topics

End-to-end modeling workflow
Data preprocessing
Model tuning
Model comparison
Practical considerations on real datasets

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
20.2 Teaching Goals
Students should be able to complete a full predictive analysis from data setup through final model comparison and interpretation.

title: 1  6-Week Course Plan (Simplified)
text:
20.3 Suggested Emphasis

Organize this unit around a full case study.
Show how preprocessing, resampling, tuning, and comparison fit together.
Emphasize reproducible workflow and communication.

title: 2  Linear Regression
text:
2.1 Quick Overview
Linear regression is the basic supervised learning method for a quantitative response. It models the mean of the response as a linear function of the predictors:
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon.
\]
Equivalently, in matrix form,
\[
y = X\beta + \varepsilon.
\]
Linear regression is popular because it is:

Definition 2.2 (Quantitative and Qualitative [3])  

Quantitative data are observations measured on a naturally occurring numerical scale. It is also called numerical data.
Qualitative data are nonnumerical data that can only be classified into one of a group of categories. It is also called categorical data.


In regression, qualitative variables must be encoded using indicator (dummy) variables.

Definition 2.3 (Discrete and Continuous [3])  

Discrete random variable: A random variable that assumes either a finite number of values or an infinite sequence of values such as 0, 1, 2…
Continuous random variable: A random variable that may assume any numerical value in an interval or collection of intervals.







graph TD
    A[All Variables] --> B[Numerical]
    A --> C[Categorical]
    B --> D[Continuous]
    B --> E[Discrete]












NoteRandom variables



A variable in a dataset can be modeled by a random variable. The probability density function / probability mass function of the random variable can describe the distribution of all possible values of the variable in a dataset.
Making a measurement is equivalent to taking a sample from the corresponding random variable. This will be discussed in detail in ?sec-inference.

2.4.1 Qualitative (categorical) data
Usually the most important is the class relative frequency: \[
\text{class relative frequency}=\frac{\text{class frequency}}{n}.
\]
To display it, we could use table, bar chart or pie chart.

The variable Pop (location where possum was trapped) is qualitative. We could use the following ways to display it. We first compute the frequency table of the variable Pop:

table(possum$Pop)


  Vic other 
   46    58 


or relative frequency table:

table(possum$Pop)/length(possum$Pop)


      Vic     other 
0.4423077 0.5576923 


Then we could draw the barplot of this variable:

barplot(table(possum$Pop))








and the pie plot:

pie(table(possum$Pop))








Note that table is handling the statistics, while barplot and pie draw on top of the result from table.




2.4.2 Quantitative (numerical) data
For quantitative data, we would like to see the histogram, as well as computing some statistics: min, max, quartiles, median and mean.

Histogram is an approximation of the distribution. In other words, we split the range into small segments (called bins), and count the frequency or relative frequency of data falling into these bins.
Box plot consists of a box, two lines and possibly some points:

The box in the box plot extends from the lower quartile to the upper quartile. The difference between the upper quartile and the lower quartile is called the inter-quartile range (IQR).
The lines, known as whiskers, extend to one and a half times the interquartile range, but they stop at the most extreme data points that fall within this range.
The points, considered as outliers, are those which are not covered by the box and the lines.


Histograms and boxplots help check normality and outliers before fitting regression. These summaries approximate the underlying distribution of the variable. In regression, we later apply similar tools to the residuals to check model assumptions.

2.4.3 Relations among multiple variables
We could show the relation between two variables in a scatterplot. Scatterplots help us assess linearity, detect outliers, and decide whether transformation is needed.

Example 2.5 (possum)  


Click to expand.

When both variables are numerical and continuous:

plot(possum$hdlngth, possum$skullw)








When one variable is categorical:

plot(as.factor(possum$Pop), possum$skullw)








Note that in this case, the categorical data has to be a factor. And once it is cast into a factor, the plot is multiple box plots for each category. This type of grouped boxplot corresponds to comparing group means and is directly related to one-way ANOVA and regression with dummy variables. It will be discussed in later sections.
We can see pair plots for each pair of variables. Pair plots are very important because they help reveal relationships among predictors. Note that before creating the plot, we have to cast pop and sex into factors.

possum$Pop <- as.factor(possum$Pop)
possum$sex <- as.factor(possum$sex)
pairs(possum)

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
19.1 Gemini
This course plan integrates the theoretical depth of The Elements of Statistical Learning (ESL) and the computational focus of Ruoqing Zhu’s SMLR and STAT 432 with the intuitive, application-based approach of An Introduction to Statistical Learning (ISLR).
The curriculum is designed for a 15-week semester, prioritizing numerical optimization (as emphasized in SMLR) alongside standard statistical learning theory.

19.1.1 Phase 1: Foundations & Optimization (Weeks 1–3)
Focus: Setting the stage with R, Linear Algebra, and the “How” of algorithm fitting. * Week 1: Introduction & The Learning Framework * What is Statistical Learning? (ISLR Ch. 2; ESL Ch. 2). * Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning. * The Bias-Variance Trade-off and MSE (SMLR Ch. 12.3). * Week 2: Computational Toolkit * R & RMarkdown: Environment setup and reproducible workflows (SMLR Ch. 1-2). * Linear Algebra Refresher: Matrix inversion, rank updates, and block matrices (SMLR Ch. 4). * Week 3: Optimization Basics * First and Second-order properties (Gradient vs. Hessian). * Algorithms: Gradient Descent, Newton’s Method, and Coordinate Descent (SMLR Ch. 5). * Lab: Implementing Linear Regression using optim() from scratch.


19.1.2 Phase 2: Linear Models & Regularization (Weeks 4–6)
Focus: Moving beyond Ordinary Least Squares to handle high-dimensional data. * Week 4: Linear Regression & Model Selection * OLS properties and limitations. * Criteria: \(C_p\), AIC, BIC, and Adjusted \(R^2\) (SMLR Ch. 6; ISLR Ch. 6). * Search: Best subset vs. Stepwise methods. * Week 5: Penalized Regression (Ridge & Lasso) * Ridge Regression: Motivation via correlated variables and convexity (SMLR Ch. 7). * Lasso: The \(L_1\) penalty, sparsity, and the soft-thresholding operator (SMLR Ch. 8). * Elastic Net: Combining \(L_1\) and \(L_2\) penalties. * Week 6: Path Algorithms & Cross-Validation * The Lasso solution path and Path-wise Coordinate Descent (SMLR Ch. 8.4). * k-fold and Leave-one-out Cross-Validation (ISLR Ch. 5). * Lab: The glmnet package in R.


19.1.3 Phase 3: Classification & Smoothing (Weeks 7–9)
Focus: Categorical outcomes and non-linear boundaries. * Week 7: Linear Classification * Logistic Regression: Parameters, interpretation, and Newton-Raphson for MLE (SMLR Ch. 10). * Discriminant Analysis (LDA/QDA): Bayes’ Rule and decision boundaries (ISLR Ch. 4; SMLR Ch. 11). * Week 8: Non-parametric Smoothing & Splines * Moving beyond linearity: Polynomials and Piecewise functions. * Splines: Basis functions, Natural Cubic Splines, and Smoothing Splines (SMLR Ch. 9; ISLR Ch. 7). * Week 9: Local Learning & Kernels * K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) for regression and classification (SMLR Ch. 12). * Kernel Density Estimation and Nadaraya-Watson (SMLR Ch. 13). * The “Curse of Dimensionality.”


19.1.4 Phase 4: Support Vector Machines & Kernels (Weeks 10–11)
Focus: The “Kernel Trick” and Maximal Margins. * Week 10: Support Vector Machines (SVM) * The Maximal Margin Classifier and Slack Variables (SMLR Ch. 17). * Duality: From Primal to Dual formulations (ESL Ch. 12). * Week 11: RKHS & Kernel Methods * Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) basics (SMLR Ch. 15). * Kernel Ridge Regression and the Kernel Trick (SMLR Ch. 16).


19.1.5 Phase 5: Trees, Ensembles & Unsupervised Learning (Weeks 12–15)
Focus: Modern “Black Box” winners and discovery without labels. * Week 12: Tree-Based Methods * Decision Trees: Gini index vs. Entropy (ISLR Ch. 8). * Bagging and Random Forests (SMLR Ch. 19-20). * Week 13: Boosting * AdaBoost and the Stagewise Additive Model. * Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM) and XGBoost/LightGBM concepts (SMLR Ch. 22-23). * Week 14: Unsupervised Learning I (Clustering) * K-means and Hierarchical Clustering (SMLR Ch. 24-25). * Spectral Clustering and the Graph Laplacian (SMLR Ch. 28). * Week 15: Unsupervised Learning II (Dimension Reduction) * Principal Component Analysis (PCA). * Modern visualization: t-SNE and UMAP (SMLR Ch. 31).


19.1.6 Required Reading List

SMLR: Zhu, R. Statistical Machine Learning with R. (Primary for optimization/computation).
ISLR: James, G., et al. An Introduction to Statistical Learning. (Primary for intuition/labs).
ESL: Hastie, T., et al. The Elements of Statistical Learning. (Reference for theoretical derivations).

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.1 A Short History of Ridge Regression
Ridge regression was introduced as a remedy for instability in ordinary least squares when predictors are highly correlated. The idea of stabilizing an inverse problem by adding a penalty term originates from Tikhonov (1963) in numerical analysis, where a regularization term was added to ill-posed problems.
In statistics, Hoerl and Kennard (1970) formally introduced ridge regression to address multicollinearity in linear regression. Later work connected ridge regression to generalized inverses, biased estimation, Bayesian regression, and other regularization methods.

title: 3  Ridge regression
section: 3.2 A Short History of Ridge Regression
text:
3.2 A Short History of Ridge Regression
Ridge regression was introduced as a remedy for instability in ordinary least squares when predictors are highly correlated. The idea of stabilizing an inverse problem by adding a penalty term originates from the work of Tikhonov (1963) in numerical analysis, where a regularization term was added to ill-posed problems.
In statistics, Hoerl and Kennard (1970) formally introduced ridge regression to address multicollinearity in linear regression. They proposed the estimator
\[
\hat{\beta}_{ridge} = (X^TX + \lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty,
\]
which shrinks coefficient estimates toward zero to reduce variance.
Later work connected ridge regression to several broader frameworks.
Marquardt (1970) discussed its relationship to generalized inverses and biased estimation. Ridge regression was also recognized as equivalent to Bayesian linear regression with a Gaussian prior on the coefficients.
In modern statistics and machine learning, ridge regression is viewed as one of the earliest and most fundamental regularization methods, forming the basis for techniques such as kernel ridge regression and influencing later developments like the LASSO (Tibshirani, 1996).

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:

Hoerl, A. E., & Kennard, R. W. (1970). Ridge regression: Biased estimation for nonorthogonal problems. Technometrics, 12(1), 55-67.
Marquardt, D. W. (1970). Generalized inverses, ridge regression, biased linear estimation, and nonlinear estimation. Technometrics, 12(3), 591-612.
Tibshirani, R. (1996). Regression shrinkage and selection via the lasso. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B, 58(1), 267-288.
Tikhonov, A. N. (1963). Solution of incorrectly formulated problems and the regularization method. Soviet Mathematics Doklady, 4, 1035-1038.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.6 Scaling Issues of Ridge Regression
Ridge regression is sensitive to the scale of the predictors, so the variables are usually standardized before fitting the model.
The usual standardization is
\[
x_j^* = \frac{x_j - \bar{x}_j}{s_j},
\]
so that each predictor has mean \(0\) and standard deviation \(1\).
This matters because the penalty acts directly on the coefficients. If predictors are measured on very different scales, then the penalty will not be applied fairly across variables.
In practice:

the predictors are standardized before fitting the model;
the intercept is typically left unpenalized;
software such as glmnet usually performs standardization automatically.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
7.1 1.1 Motivation: Why Regularization
Students should first understand the limitations of ordinary least squares.
Key issues include:

Overfitting
High variance of coefficient estimates
Multicollinearity
Poor prediction performance when predictors are highly correlated

Introduce the bias–variance tradeoff.
Explain that instead of minimizing only residual sum of squares
\[
RSS = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
\]
we add a penalty term to control model complexity.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
7.2 1.2 Ridge Regression
Ridge regression solves
\[
\min_{\beta}
RSS + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2
\]
where

\(\lambda \ge 0\) controls the strength of regularization.

Key ideas students should understand:

coefficients are shrunk toward zero
ridge does not set coefficients exactly to zero
useful when predictors are highly correlated
reduces variance of estimates

Important properties:

When \(\lambda = 0\), ridge reduces to OLS
As \(\lambda \to \infty\), coefficients approach zero

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
7.3 1.3 Lasso Regression
Lasso regression solves
\[
\min_{\beta}
RSS + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j|
\]
Key properties:

some coefficients become exactly zero
performs automatic variable selection
produces sparse models

This makes Lasso useful for interpretability and feature selection.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
7.4 1.4 Geometric Interpretation
Students should understand the geometry behind Ridge and Lasso.
Ridge constraint:
\[
\sum \beta_j^2 \le t
\]
This produces a circular constraint region.
Lasso constraint:
\[
\sum |\beta_j| \le t
\]
This produces a diamond-shaped region.
Because the diamond has corners, the solution often occurs at axes where coefficients equal zero. This explains why Lasso performs variable selection.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
7.5 1.5 Standardizing Predictors
Before applying Ridge or Lasso, predictors should be standardized.
Reason:

the penalty depends on coefficient magnitude
variables with large scale would otherwise be penalized differently

Typical preprocessing:
\[
x_j \leftarrow \frac{x_j - \bar{x}_j}{sd(x_j)}
\]
Most software automatically performs this step.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
7.6 1.6 Choosing the Tuning Parameter
The tuning parameter \(\lambda\) determines the strength of regularization.
A common method is K-fold cross-validation.
Procedure:

Fit models across a grid of \(\lambda\)
Compute validation error
Choose \(\lambda\) minimizing CV error

In practice, two common choices are

\(\lambda_{min}\): minimum CV error
\(\lambda_{1se}\): simplest model within one standard error

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
7.7 1.7 Practical Implementation
Students should see how to fit these models in software.
Example in R using glmnet:
library(glmnet)

x <- model.matrix(y ~ ., data)[,-1]
y <- data$y

ridge_fit <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)
lasso_fit <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)
Cross-validation:
cv_fit <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)
plot(cv_fit)
Coefficient path:
plot(lasso_fit)

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
8.1 2.1 Elastic Net
Elastic Net combines Ridge and Lasso penalties:
\[
\lambda_1 \sum |\beta_j| + \lambda_2 \sum \beta_j^2
\]
Purpose:

handles groups of correlated predictors
provides a compromise between Ridge and Lasso

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
8.2 2.2 Relation to Subset Selection
Explain the relationship between regularization and model selection.



Method
Penalty




Best subset
\(||\beta||_0\)


Ridge
\(||\beta||_2\)


Lasso
\(||\beta||_1\)



This helps students understand different approaches to controlling model complexity.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
8.3 2.3 Effective Degrees of Freedom
For ridge regression, the effective degrees of freedom can be written as
\[
df(\lambda) = tr(S_\lambda)
\]
This concept helps connect ridge regression with smoothing methods.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
8.4 2.4 Bayesian Interpretation
Regularization can also be viewed from a Bayesian perspective.

Ridge regression corresponds to a Gaussian prior on coefficients
Lasso corresponds to a Laplace prior

This connection is conceptually interesting but not required.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
9.1 3.1 Optimization Algorithms
Detailed derivations of algorithms such as:

Coordinate descent
Least Angle Regression (LARS)
Convex optimization proofs

Students only need to know that efficient algorithms exist.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
9.2 3.2 High-Dimensional Statistical Theory
Topics such as:

sparsistency
irrepresentable conditions
oracle properties

are usually covered only in advanced statistics courses.

title: 5  Ridge regression
text:
9.3 3.3 Full Mathematical Derivation
The ridge estimator
\[
\hat{\beta} = (X^T X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^T y
\]
can be shown, but a full derivation is not necessary for most students.

title: 2  Linear Regression
text:
2.7 Model Selection
In multiple regression, several competing predictor sets may be plausible. Model selection compares them using a criterion that balances fit and complexity.

2.7.1 AIC, BIC, and Mallows’ \(C_p\)
These criteria reward good fit but penalize model complexity.

AIC tends to prefer somewhat larger models.
BIC penalizes complexity more strongly and often prefers smaller models.
Mallows’ \(C_p\) compares a candidate model to a larger reference model.

These tools are useful for comparing models, but they should not replace subject-matter reasoning.


2.7.2 Best Subset Selection
Best subset selection compares all models of a given size and then chooses the best one according to a criterion such as AIC, BIC, adjusted \(R^2\), or \(C_p\).
It is conceptually simple, but it becomes computationally expensive when the number of predictors is large.


2.7.3 Stepwise Selection
Stepwise procedures add or remove variables sequentially rather than searching all possible models.
Common versions are:

forward selection,
backward elimination,
bidirectional stepwise selection.

These methods are faster than best subset selection, but they are greedy procedures and do not guarantee the globally best model.






ImportantCaution



If many models are tried and the final model is selected from the same data, p-values and confidence intervals from the final selected model should be interpreted cautiously.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.1 What Is LASSO?
The LASSO is a regularized version of linear regression that adds an \(L_1\) penalty to the least-squares criterion. Its name stands for least absolute shrinkage and selection operator.
For a linear model with response vector \(y\) and design matrix \(X\), the LASSO estimator is defined by
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\mathrm{lasso}}
= \arg\min_{\beta}
\left\{
\lVert y - X\beta \rVert_2^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j|
\right\},
\]
where \(\lambda \ge 0\) is a tuning parameter.
When \(\lambda = 0\), the method reduces to ordinary least squares. As \(\lambda\) increases, the penalty becomes stronger and the coefficients are shrunk more aggressively.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.2 What Is Ridge Regression?
Ridge regression is a regularized version of ordinary least squares (OLS). It is especially useful when predictors are highly correlated or when the matrix \(X^\top X\) is nearly singular, because in such settings the OLS estimator can be unstable.
Recall the linear model
\[
y = X\beta + \varepsilon.
\]
When \(X^\top X\) is invertible, the OLS estimator is
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\mathrm{OLS}} = (X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top y.
\]
Ridge regression modifies the least-squares criterion by adding a quadratic penalty:
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\mathrm{ridge}}
= \arg\min_{\beta}
\left\{
\lVert y - X\beta \rVert_2^2 + \lambda \lVert \beta \rVert_2^2
\right\},
\]
where \(\lambda \ge 0\) is a tuning parameter.
Equivalently,
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\mathrm{ridge}} = (X^\top X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^\top y.
\]
The penalty shrinks coefficients toward zero. Larger values of \(\lambda\) produce stronger shrinkage.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
prediction: accurately predict the response for new observations;
inference or explanation: understand how the predictors are related to the response.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.3 Regression and Classification
Within supervised learning, two major tasks are:

regression, where the response is quantitative;
classification, where the response is categorical.

For example:

predicting salary is a regression problem;
predicting whether a tumor is benign or malignant is a classification problem.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.4 Prediction Versus Inference
Prediction and inference are related, but they are not the same.
If the goal is prediction, we care mainly about how well the model performs on new data. A more complex model may be acceptable if it improves predictive accuracy.
If the goal is inference, we care about understanding the role of each predictor. In this case, interpretability is often more important than pure predictive performance.






NoteMain Point



The best predictive model is not always the best explanatory model. Statistical learning often requires choosing between interpretability and flexibility.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.5 Parametric and Nonparametric Methods
A parametric method assumes a specific functional form for \(f\). For example, linear regression assumes that
\[
f(X) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p.
\]
This assumption simplifies estimation and interpretation, but it may be too restrictive.
A nonparametric method does not assume a fixed functional form. It is more flexible, but it usually requires more data and may be harder to interpret.
Thus:

parametric models are simpler and easier to interpret;
nonparametric models are more flexible but may have higher variance.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.6 Training Error and Test Error
When we fit a model, we can measure how well it matches the observed data. This is the training error.
However, what really matters is performance on new observations. This is the test error.
A model can have very small training error but still perform poorly on new data. This phenomenon is called overfitting.

Underfitting occurs when the model is too simple to capture the signal.
Overfitting occurs when the model is too flexible and starts fitting noise.

Good statistical learning aims to minimize test error, not merely training error.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.7 Bias-Variance Tradeoff
A central idea in statistical learning is the bias-variance tradeoff.

A simple model tends to have higher bias but lower variance.
A flexible model tends to have lower bias but higher variance.

As model flexibility increases:

training error usually decreases;
test error often decreases at first, then increases once overfitting begins.

The goal is to find a level of complexity that balances bias and variance well.






ImportantWhy This Matters



Many modern methods can be understood as ways to manage the bias-variance tradeoff. Regularization, cross-validation, and model comparison are all tools for this purpose.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
text:
1.8 A Typical Statistical Learning Workflow
A practical workflow often looks like this:

Define the prediction or explanation goal.
Explore and preprocess the data.
Choose one or more candidate models.
Estimate model performance using validation or cross-validation.
Compare models and select an appropriate final model.
Interpret results and communicate conclusions.

This course will revisit this workflow repeatedly with different methods.

title: 1  Statistical Learning Basics
2  Linear Regression
text:
2.2 The Ordinary Least Squares Estimator
The most common fitting method is ordinary least squares (OLS). It chooses the coefficient vector \(\beta\) that minimizes the residual sum of squares:
\[
\mathrm{RSS}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
= \lVert y - X\beta \rVert_2^2.
\]
The OLS estimator is
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\mathrm{OLS}} = (X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top y,
\]
provided that \(X^\top X\) is invertible.
Once the coefficients are estimated, the fitted value for observation \(i\) is
\[
\hat{y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_{i1} + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_p x_{ip}.
\]

title: 2  Linear Regression
2  Linear Regression
section: 2.4 Categorical Variables, Interactions, and Nonlinear Terms
text:
2.4 Categorical Variables, Interactions, and Nonlinear Terms
Linear regression can handle more than purely numeric additive effects.

2.4.1 Categorical Predictors
Categorical predictors are represented through indicator variables. If a factor has \(k\) levels, the model usually includes \(k-1\) dummy variables.
Thus, linear regression can compare group means while adjusting for other predictors.


2.4.2 Interaction Terms
An interaction allows the effect of one predictor to depend on another predictor. For example,
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_3 x_1 x_2 + \varepsilon.
\]
If \(\beta_3 \neq 0\), the effect of \(x_1\) depends on the value of \(x_2\).


2.4.3 Polynomial and Transformed Terms
We may also include nonlinear transformations such as:

\(x^2\),
\(\log(x)\),
\(\sqrt{x}\).

The model remains a linear model because it is still linear in the coefficients \(\beta\).

title: 2  Linear Regression
text:
2.5 Interpreting the Model
Interpretation is one of the main strengths of linear regression.

2.5.1 Coefficients
The coefficient \(\beta_j\) represents the change in the mean response associated with a one-unit increase in \(X_j\), holding the other predictors fixed.


2.5.2 Main Effects With Interactions
When interactions are present, a main effect should not be interpreted in isolation. Its meaning depends on the interacting variables.


2.5.3 Confidence Intervals
A confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for a coefficient or for the mean response at a given predictor combination.


2.5.4 Prediction Intervals
A prediction interval is wider than a confidence interval because it must account for both:

uncertainty in the estimated mean;
random variation in a future observation.

title: 2  Linear Regression
text:
2.6 Assessing Model Fit
Several tools are commonly used to assess a linear regression fit:

residual plots,
Q-Q plots,
leverage and influence diagnostics,
measures such as \(R^2\), adjusted \(R^2\), and residual standard error.

Residual plots help detect:

nonlinearity,
nonconstant variance,
outliers,
influential observations.

These diagnostics do not prove that a model is correct, but they help identify important problems.

title: 2  Linear Regression
3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.1 What Is a GLM?
A generalized linear model (GLM) extends ordinary linear regression in two ways:
A GLM has three components:
\[
\eta = X\beta.
\]
\[
g(\mu) = \eta = X\beta.
\]
Thus, a GLM keeps the predictor linear in the coefficients, but it allows the response distribution and the mean-response relationship to be more flexible than in ordinary linear regression.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
the response variable does not need to be normally distributed;
the mean of the response is connected to the predictors through a link function.



A response distribution from the exponential family, such as Gaussian, Binomial, or Poisson.
A linear predictor



A link function relating the mean \(\mu = \mathbb{E}(Y \mid X)\) to the linear predictor:









NoteMain Idea



Linear regression models the response directly under a Gaussian error assumption. A GLM models the mean of the response through a link function and can handle binary, count, and other non-Gaussian outcomes.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.2 Why Do We Need GLMs?
Ordinary linear regression is not always appropriate.

For binary outcomes, fitted values from linear regression may fall outside \([0,1]\).
For count data, the response is nonnegative and often right-skewed.
The variance may depend on the mean, which violates the constant-variance assumption of linear regression.

GLMs address these issues by choosing a distribution and a link function suited to the data.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.3 Common Examples

3.3.1 Linear Regression
For a Gaussian response with the identity link,
\[
\mu = X\beta.
\]
This is the ordinary linear model.


3.3.2 Logistic Regression
For binary data, logistic regression uses
\[
\log\left(\frac{\mu}{1-\mu}\right) = X\beta,
\]
where \(\mu = \mathbb{P}(Y=1 \mid X)\).


3.3.3 Poisson Regression
For count data, Poisson regression uses
\[
\log(\mu) = X\beta,
\]
where \(\mu = \mathbb{E}(Y \mid X)\).

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.4 Relation to Linear Regression
A GLM is not the same as transforming the response and then applying ordinary least squares.
For example,
\[
\log(Y) = X\beta + \varepsilon
\]
is a transformed linear model, whereas
\[
\log(\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X]) = X\beta
\]
is a GLM with a log link.
These models may look similar algebraically, but they differ in assumptions, interpretation, and estimation.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.6 Interpretation
The coefficients in a GLM are interpreted through the link function.

In logistic regression, coefficients describe changes in log-odds.
In Poisson regression, coefficients describe changes in the log mean count.
In linear regression, coefficients describe direct changes in the mean response.

Therefore, interpretation depends on both the response distribution and the link.

title: 3  Generalized linear models
text:
3.7 Relation to GAMs and Regularization
GLMs still assume that the predictor enters through the linear form \(X\beta\). A generalized additive model (GAM) relaxes this by replacing linear terms with smooth functions:
\[
g(\mu) = \beta_0 + f_1(x_1) + \cdots + f_p(x_p).
\]
Regularization methods such as ridge regression and LASSO can also be combined with GLMs, producing penalized logistic regression, penalized Poisson regression, and related models.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.1 What Is a GAM?
A generalized additive model (GAM) extends a generalized linear model by allowing the effect of a predictor to be a smooth function rather than a fixed linear term.
Instead of writing
\[
g(\mu) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_p x_p,
\]
a GAM writes
\[
g(\mu) = \beta_0 + f_1(x_1) + \cdots + f_p(x_p),
\]
where each \(f_j\) is a smooth function estimated from the data.
Here \(\mu = \mathbb{E}(Y \mid X)\) and \(g\) is a link function, just as in a GLM.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
NoteMain Idea



A GLM assumes a specific parametric form for each predictor effect. A GAM allows the shape of the effect to be learned from the data, subject to a smoothness constraint.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.2 Why Do We Need GAMs?
In practice, relationships between predictors and response are often nonlinear.
In ordinary linear regression, one may try transformations such as:

\(\log(x)\),
\(\sqrt{x}\),
\(x^2\).

These are still parametric choices made by the analyst. A GAM provides a more flexible alternative by estimating a smooth curve directly from the data.
This is useful when:

the true relationship is nonlinear;
the correct transformation is not known in advance;
we want flexibility without giving up interpretability completely.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
section: 4.3 Relation to Linear Regression and GLMs

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.4 Smooth Functions
The functions \(f_j\) are not arbitrary. They are usually represented by basis expansions, such as splines, and are estimated with a smoothness penalty.
The purpose of the penalty is to prevent the fitted curve from becoming too wiggly.
Thus, a GAM balances:

fidelity to the data,
smoothness of the fitted functions.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.5 GAM Versus Manual Transformations
Suppose in a linear model you try
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \log(x) + \varepsilon
\]
or
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + \varepsilon.
\]
These are still linear models with chosen transformations.
In a GAM, by contrast, we write
\[
Y = \beta_0 + f(x) + \varepsilon
\]
or, more generally,
\[
g(\mu) = \beta_0 + f(x).
\]
The difference is that the shape of \(f\) is estimated from the data rather than fixed in advance as \(\log(x)\) or \(x^2\).

title: 4  Generalized additive models
text:
4.6 Estimation and Inference
GAMs are usually fitted by penalized likelihood methods. The amount of smoothness is controlled by smoothing parameters, which are often selected automatically by criteria such as:

generalized cross-validation (GCV),
restricted maximum likelihood (REML),
cross-validation.

Because the model learns functional form from the data, inference should still be interpreted with care, especially if the analyst repeatedly changes the model after inspecting diagnostic plots.

title: 4  Generalized additive models
5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.3 Why Ridge Regression Helps
OLS is unbiased under the classical linear model assumptions, but it can have high variance when predictors are strongly correlated. Ridge regression introduces bias, but it often reduces variance enough to improve predictive performance.
This is a standard example of the bias-variance tradeoff.






NoteKey Properties




If \(\lambda = 0\), ridge regression reduces to OLS.
If \(\lambda > 0\), the matrix \(X^\top X + \lambda I\) is invertible even when \(X^\top X\) is singular.
Ridge shrinks coefficients continuously toward zero.
Ridge usually does not set coefficients exactly to zero.
Ridge is particularly useful under multicollinearity.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.4 Choosing \(\lambda\)
The tuning parameter \(\lambda\) determines how much shrinkage is applied.

Small \(\lambda\) gives a model close to OLS.
Large \(\lambda\) gives stronger regularization.

In practice, the modern standard is to choose \(\lambda\) by cross-validation. If \(\mathrm{CV}(\lambda)\) denotes the cross-validation error, we choose
\[
\hat{\lambda}
= \arg\min_{\lambda} \mathrm{CV}(\lambda).
\]
Classical ridge analysis also uses ridge trace plots and generalized cross-validation.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.5 Fitting Ridge Regression in R
The glmnet package is a standard implementation:
library(glmnet)

x <- model.matrix(y ~ ., data = dat)[, -1]
y <- dat$y

ridge_fit <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)
plot(ridge_fit)
Cross-validation is performed with:
cv_fit <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)
plot(cv_fit)
coef(cv_fit, s = "lambda.min")
Here alpha = 0 specifies ridge regression.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
text:
5.7 Relation to Tikhonov Regularization
Ridge regression is the statistical form of Tikhonov regularization. In inverse problems, Tikhonov regularization stabilizes an ill-posed problem by adding a quadratic penalty. Ridge regression applies the same idea to linear regression.

title: 5  Ridge Regression
6  LASSO
text:
NoteMain Point



Like ridge regression, LASSO controls model complexity by shrinking coefficients. Unlike ridge regression, it can set some coefficients exactly equal to zero, so it also performs variable selection.

title: 6  LASSO
6  LASSO
text:
6.3 Variable Selection and Shrinkage
The defining feature of LASSO is that the \(L_1\) penalty can force some coefficient estimates to be exactly zero.
As a result:

unimportant variables may be removed from the model;
the final model may be easier to interpret;
prediction error may improve if irrelevant variables are excluded.

Ridge regression also shrinks coefficients, but it usually keeps all variables in the model. LASSO shrinks and selects at the same time.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.4 Geometric Interpretation
The geometric explanation of LASSO is useful for understanding why exact zeros occur.
Ridge regression can be written with an \(L_2\) constraint:
\[
\sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 \le t.
\]
The corresponding constraint region is a circle in two dimensions, or a sphere in higher dimensions.
LASSO can be written with an \(L_1\) constraint:
\[
\sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| \le t.
\]
The corresponding constraint region is a diamond in two dimensions.
Because the diamond has corners on the coordinate axes, the least-squares contours often touch the constraint region at a corner. At such a point, one or more coefficients are exactly zero.






ImportantComparison With Ridge



Ridge has a smooth constraint boundary, so coefficients are typically shrunk but remain nonzero. LASSO has corners aligned with the axes, so solutions often occur at points where some coefficients are exactly zero.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.5 Bias-Variance Tradeoff
LASSO is a biased estimator. The penalty pulls coefficients toward zero, so the estimates are no longer unbiased in the classical sense.
However, this bias can be worthwhile because it may greatly reduce variance. If the reduction in variance is large enough, the overall prediction error can decrease.
Thus, LASSO is another example of the bias-variance tradeoff:

less flexibility may increase bias;
less flexibility may also reduce variance;
prediction may improve if the tradeoff is favorable.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.6 How to Choose \(\lambda\)
The tuning parameter \(\lambda\) controls the amount of regularization in both ridge regression and LASSO.

When \(\lambda = 0\), both methods reduce to ordinary least squares.
As \(\lambda\) increases, the coefficients are shrunk more strongly.
In ridge regression, coefficients move continuously toward zero.
In LASSO, some coefficients may become exactly zero.

So the practical question is: how should we choose \(\lambda\)?






NoteMain Idea



There is no single universally best value of \(\lambda\). It is usually chosen to balance model fit and model complexity, with the goal of improving prediction and avoiding overfitting.



6.6.1 A Simple Way
The simplest explanation is to treat \(\lambda\) as a knob that controls the strength of shrinkage.

A small \(\lambda\) gives a model close to least squares.
A large \(\lambda\) gives a more heavily regularized model.

In an introductory discussion, one can fit the model over a grid of \(\lambda\) values and inspect how the coefficients change. This gives an immediate visual understanding of the regularization path.
For ridge regression, the coefficients decrease smoothly as \(\lambda\) grows. For LASSO, some coefficients drop to zero as \(\lambda\) increases.


6.6.2 The Traditional Classical Way
A classical approach is to choose \(\lambda\) using an analytic criterion that balances goodness of fit against model complexity.
For ridge regression, older literature often used:

inspection of ridge trace plots,
Mallows’ \(C_p\),
Akaike’s Information Criterion (AIC),
Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC),
generalized cross-validation (GCV).

For LASSO, information criteria such as AIC or BIC can also be used, usually together with an estimate of the effective degrees of freedom.


6.6.3 The Modern Way
The modern standard is to choose \(\lambda\) by cross-validation, especially \(K\)-fold cross-validation.
If \(\mathrm{CV}(\lambda)\) denotes the cross-validation error, we choose
\[
\hat{\lambda}
= \arg\min_{\lambda} \mathrm{CV}(\lambda).
\]
In practice, two values are often reported:

\(\lambda_{\min}\), the value that minimizes cross-validation error;
\(\lambda_{1se}\), the largest value of \(\lambda\) within one standard error of the minimum.

Usually, \(\lambda_{\min}\) gives the most accurate prediction, while \(\lambda_{1se}\) gives a simpler and more stable model.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.7 Standardization
For both ridge regression and LASSO, the predictors are usually standardized before fitting the model.
The usual standardization is
\[
x_j^* = \frac{x_j - \bar{x}_j}{s_j},
\]
so that each predictor has mean \(0\) and standard deviation \(1\).
This is important because the penalty acts on the coefficient magnitudes. Without standardization, variables measured on different scales would be penalized unequally.
In practice:

we usually standardize the predictors \(X\);
we usually do not penalize the intercept;
software such as glmnet standardizes predictors automatically by default.

title: 6  LASSO
text:
6.8 Fitting LASSO With glmnet
In R, the most common implementation is the glmnet package.
library(glmnet)

x <- model.matrix(y ~ ., data = dat)[, -1]
y <- dat$y

lasso_fit <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)
plot(lasso_fit)
To choose \(\lambda\) by cross-validation:
cv_fit <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)
plot(cv_fit)

coef(cv_fit, s = "lambda.min")
coef(cv_fit, s = "lambda.1se")
Here:

alpha = 1 specifies LASSO;
lambda.min gives the model with minimum CV error;
lambda.1se gives a simpler model within one standard error of the minimum.

title: 6  LASSO
section: 6.9 Relation to Forward Stagewise Regression and Elastic Net
text:
6.9 Relation to Forward Stagewise Regression and Elastic Net
LASSO is closely related to forward stagewise regression because both build a model gradually and often produce similar coefficient paths.
LASSO is also closely related to the elastic net, which combines the ridge and LASSO penalties:
\[
\lambda_1 \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| + \lambda_2 \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2.
\]
Elastic net is often useful when predictors are highly correlated, because it combines variable selection with additional shrinkage.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Teaching Goals
Students should be able to complete a full predictive analysis from data setup through final model comparison and interpretation. ## Suggested Emphasis - Organize this unit around a full case study. - Show how preprocessing, resampling, tuning, and comparison fit together. - Emphasize reproducible workflow and communication.

title: Statistical Learning

foundations of statistical learning,
linear regression,
nonparametric regression,
early classification concepts.

title: Statistical Learning

classification,

title: Statistical Learning

Suggested format:

a real dataset,
several competing methods,
model comparison using validation or cross-validation,
written interpretation of results.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
Theme 1: Model Flexibility
Students should repeatedly ask: > How flexible is this model, and what does that flexibility buy us? ## Theme 2: Generalization Students should repeatedly ask: > How do we know whether this model will work on new data? ## Theme 3: Comparison Students should repeatedly ask: > Is this method better than what we already had? # Recommended Course Message to Students A useful recurring message for students is: > Statistical learning is about building models that perform well on new data, understanding the tradeoff between simplicity and flexibility, and choosing methods based on predictive performance. # Condensed Teaching Calendar
